cities.name: Barrington
cities.intro:
Barrington is a Lake County community west of Lake Zurich and north of Hoffman Estates, with a footprint that includes wooded subdivisions, equestrian properties, and rural lots. The terrain was shaped by the last glaciation, which left kettle holes, oak savanna remnants, and patchy heavy clay over deep-buried sand and gravel. North Barrington and the area around Cuba Marsh feature wetland-adjacent properties where drainage and ground-water levels become real factors in landscape and irrigation work. Tower Lake sits within the village footprint and shapes the surrounding microclimate.

The village core preserves a notable concentration of brick farmhouse and traditional New England-style homes, where outdoor lighting and seasonal holiday lighting are visible elements of the streetscape. Many of the larger homes in Barrington and North Barrington were built or expanded in the 1990s, and the brick and flagstone paver patios from that era are now in the window where polymeric re-sanding and edge stabilization commonly come up. Mature plantings and tree canopies through the older subdivisions favor uplighting and pathlighting approaches over driveway-edge strips.

cities.name: Crystal Lake
cities.intro:
Crystal Lake is the largest community in McHenry County, situated around the glacial lake of the same name. The geology shifts noticeably with proximity to the water: properties closest to the lake — through the Crystal Lake South area, around the Park District beach, and into adjacent Lakewood — sit on glacial outwash sand that drains quickly. Further from the lake, in Veterans Acres and the older subdivisions toward the Route 14 corridor, the soil returns to the clay-loam more typical of inland McHenry County. That difference influences irrigation runtime: sandy soils favor frequent, shorter cycles, while clay-loam favors deeper and less frequent watering.

Crystal Lake's housing stock is predominantly 1980s through 2010s mid-size suburban — different in character from the estate-heavy markets along the North Shore. Residential sprinkler systems on quarter- to half-acre lots, holiday lighting on rooflines and front entries, and paver patios and driveway aprons are common features of the local outdoor work landscape. Properties backing the conservation easements and wooded sections of the Park District are well-suited to landscape lighting that emphasizes specimen trees against natural backdrops.

cities.name: Deerfield
cities.intro:
Deerfield is a Lake County village inland from the lakefront communities, south of Riverwoods and west of Highland Park. The terrain is flat to gently rolling, without the ravines that define Highland Park and Lake Forest to the east, and without the lake-effect cooling that delays spring frost there. Deerfield's soils are clay-loam through most of the village.

Deerfield's housing stock is predominantly mid-20th-century suburban on lots typically larger than the tight North Shore villages, with newer construction concentrated north of Lake Cook Road. Outdoor work in Deerfield tends toward residential sprinkler systems on larger suburban lots, holiday lighting on rooflines and front entries, and paver patios behind houses rather than long bluestone walkways. The Deerfield Park District properties scattered through the village shape adjacent residential lighting and irrigation projects.

cities.name: Evanston
cities.intro:
Evanston is the largest Cook County city on the North Shore, situated along Lake Michigan immediately south of Wilmette and bordered by Chicago to the south. Home to Northwestern University, Evanston's housing stock mixes Victorian, Prairie School, and early-20th-century estates with substantial mid-century apartment buildings and newer mixed-use development around the downtown core. The geological pattern continues that of the rest of the North Shore — clay-loam soils, ravines draining east toward the lake, and lake-cooled spring temperatures that delay frost dissipation later than in inland Cook County.

Established neighborhoods along the lakefront, the streets near the Northwestern campus, and the older Tudor and Victorian blocks of central Evanston feature mature oak and elm canopies that shape outdoor lighting design. Brick and bluestone paver hardscape installed in the early-to-mid 20th century is common across the older sections of the city, and freeze–thaw cycles have placed many of those installations into the polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing window. The denser footprint compared to villages further north means tighter irrigation zones and more compact landscape lighting installations.

cities.name: Glencoe
cities.intro:
Glencoe is a Cook County village along Lake Michigan, between Highland Park to the north and Winnetka to the south, and home to the Chicago Botanic Garden along its western edge. The geology continues the Lake Michigan bluff pattern — ravines draining east, mature canopies, and clay-loam soils that influence both turf health and irrigation zone design. Spring frost typically lingers later than in inland Cook County because of the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on overnight low temperatures.

Glencoe's housing stock includes an unusual concentration of Frank Lloyd Wright Prairie School designs — the Ravine Bluffs subdivision contains five Wright-designed homes plus a Wright-designed bridge — alongside the Tudor, Georgian, and shingle-style homes typical of the central North Shore. The dense tree canopy through South Glencoe and the older residential streets shapes outdoor lighting design: tree uplighting and pathlights woven through mature plantings rather than driveway-edge strips. Brick and bluestone paver walks are common throughout the village's older sections and reach the polymeric joint re-sanding window after the first decade of freeze–thaw exposure.

cities.name: Glenview
cities.intro:
Glenview is a Cook County village inland from the Lake Michigan shore, southwest of Northbrook and east of Wheeling. Like its Northbrook neighbor, the terrain is flat to gently rolling without the ravines that define the lakefront villages. The lake-effect cooling that delays spring frost in Highland Park or Glencoe doesn't reach this far inland, which means irrigation start-ups can typically begin earlier here than in the bluff communities. Soils are clay-loam through most of the village.

Glenview's housing stock spans early-to-mid-20th-century established neighborhoods through to newer construction at The Glen on the former Glenview Naval Air Station site. The mix of older village and newer planned-community housing creates a varied irrigation profile — older homes on smaller lots favor compact zone designs, while The Glen and nearby newer subdivisions support more elaborate sprinkler and landscape lighting layouts. Outdoor lighting demand splits between traditional roofline holiday installations and architectural landscape lighting on the larger newer-build properties.

cities.name: Highland Park
cities.intro:
Highland Park is a Lake County community along Lake Michigan, between Lake Forest to the north and Glencoe to the south. The geology mirrors that of Lake Forest: ravines draining east toward the lake, clay-loam soils, and mature oak and elm canopies through the older neighborhoods. Spring frost typically lingers later than in inland Lake County because of Lake Michigan's moderating effect on overnight low temperatures. The Ravinia and Briar Wood areas, and bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road, sit within the same ravine-shaped terrain that defines the rest of the central North Shore.

The Ravinia neighborhood — anchored by the Ravinia Festival pavilion — sees substantial seasonal traffic between summer and the holiday season, which raises the visibility of outdoor lighting on residences along Lake Cook Road and St. Johns Avenue. Brick and bluestone paver driveways are common throughout the older sections of town and reach the polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing window after the first decade or so of freeze–thaw exposure. Architectural styles range from early-20th-century Tudor and Colonial Revival through mid-century modern, with corresponding variation in outdoor lighting approaches.

cities.name: Highwood
cities.intro:
Highwood is a small Lake County city along Lake Michigan, between Highland Park to the south and Lake Forest / Fort Sheridan to the north. Smaller in footprint and population than its bluff neighbors, Highwood has historically had a denser housing stock and a more compact commercial core anchored by Sheridan Road. The terrain shares the geology of the central North Shore — ravines draining east toward the lake, clay-loam soils, and lake-cooled spring temperatures that delay frost dissipation later than in inland Lake County.

Highwood's residential properties tend toward smaller lots than Lake Forest or Highland Park, with a denser arrangement of houses set on tighter footprints. The compact lot pattern shapes outdoor work: irrigation zones are tighter, landscape lighting leans toward house-wash and entry illumination rather than long-driveway estate lighting, and brick or paver hardscape work concentrates on patios and walks rather than long driveways. Highwood's downtown along Sheridan Road is recognized for its concentration of Italian-American restaurants — a heritage dating to the early 20th century — and the seasonal lighting along that corridor is a notable winter feature of the city.

cities.name: Kenilworth
cities.intro:
Kenilworth is the smallest of the North Shore villages by population — a Cook County community south of Winnetka and north of Wilmette, occupying a tight footprint along Lake Michigan. Founded as a planned community by Joseph Sears in the 1890s, the village's architectural character is unusually consistent: Prairie School, Colonial Revival, and traditional shingle-style homes set on large, well-shaded lots. The geology mirrors the rest of the North Shore — clay-loam soils, ravines draining east toward the lake, and lake-cooled spring temperatures that delay frost dissipation later than in inland Cook County.

Kenilworth has no commercial district; the village is almost entirely residential, which concentrates outdoor work on the homes themselves — irrigation, landscape lighting, and paver maintenance rather than commercial property work. The traditional architecture and large mature canopies through the village support both estate-scale landscape lighting and traditional holiday lighting. Brick and bluestone hardscape installed in the early-to-mid 20th century is common across the village, and freeze–thaw cycles have placed many of those installations into the polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing window.

cities.name: Lake Bluff
cities.intro:
Lake Bluff is a small Lake County village along Lake Michigan, immediately north of Lake Forest. Like its larger neighbors along the bluff, the terrain is shaped by ravines that drain east toward the lake, mature oak and elm canopies, and clay-loam soils with pockets of slow drainage near the ravine edges that influence both turf health and irrigation zone design. Spring frost typically lingers later than in inland Lake County because of the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on overnight low temperatures.

The village core around the historic Lake Bluff Beach features traditional New England-style and Tudor architecture, on which traditional holiday lighting is a recognizable seasonal staple. Properties along Sheridan Road and the bluff-edge sit within the same ravine-shaped terrain that defines the rest of the central North Shore. Brick and bluestone paver hardscape is common across the older sections of the village and reaches the polymeric joint re-sanding and sealing window after the first decade or so of freeze–thaw exposure.

cities.name: Lake Forest
cities.intro:
Lake Forest is a Lake County community along the Lake Michigan bluffs, between Lake Bluff to the north and Highwood to the south. The landscape is shaped by ravines that drain east toward the lake, mature oak and elm canopies, and large lots throughout the central and east neighborhoods. Soils tend toward heavy clay-loam, with pockets of poor drainage near the ravine edges that influence both turf health and irrigation zone design. Spring frost typically lingers later in Lake Forest than in inland Lake or McHenry counties because of the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on overnight low temperatures.

Onwentsia, Knollwood, and the bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road are characterized by estate lots, long driveways, and gated entries — settings where landscape lighting is a substantial part of the local outdoor design vocabulary. The downtown core around Market Square is anchored by Tudor and Georgian architecture, on which traditional holiday lighting is a recognizable seasonal staple. Bluestone walkways and clay-brick paver driveways are common throughout central Lake Forest, particularly in older sections where freeze–thaw cycles eventually call for polymeric joint re-sanding and edge stabilization.

cities.name: Northbrook
cities.intro:
Northbrook is a Cook County village inland from the Lake Michigan shore, between Highland Park to the east and Glenview to the south. Unlike the bluff villages, Northbrook's terrain is comparatively flat — without ravines and without the cooling lake effect on overnight temperatures, which means spring frost lifts earlier here than in Highland Park or Glencoe. The soils are clay-loam through most of the village, with somewhat better drainage than the heavier clay pockets along the bluff edge.

Northbrook's housing stock is predominantly mid-20th-century suburban, with newer estate construction concentrated along the western and northern edges of the village. The combination of larger lots and a flatter grade gives Northbrook a different irrigation profile than the bluff communities: longer pipe runs and more heads per zone, with designs that don't have to navigate ravine drainage. Outdoor lighting demand here splits between traditional roofline holiday lighting on suburban tracts and architectural landscape lighting on the larger newer-build estates.

cities.name: Wilmette
cities.intro:
Wilmette is a Cook County village along Lake Michigan, immediately south of Kenilworth and north of Evanston. Distinct from the smaller, more uniformly residential villages to its north, Wilmette has a more diverse housing stock and a larger commercial core along Central Street and around Plaza del Lago. The geological pattern continues that of the rest of the North Shore: ravines draining east toward the lake, clay-loam soils that influence both turf health and irrigation zone design, and lake-cooled spring temperatures that delay frost dissipation later than in inland Cook County.

The Bahá'í House of Worship — the only one in North America — sits at the eastern edge of the village along Sheridan Road, drawing visitors year-round; the surrounding Linden Avenue and Sheridan corridors carry an unusually high concentration of formal landscaping. The older neighborhoods through central and west Wilmette feature mature oak and elm canopies that shape outdoor lighting design — uplighting and pathlights woven through established plantings rather than driveway-edge strips. Brick and bluestone paver installations through these older sections reach the polymeric joint re-sanding window after the first decade of freeze–thaw exposure.

cities.name: Winnetka
cities.intro:
Winnetka is a Cook County village along Lake Michigan, bordered by Glencoe to the north and Kenilworth to the south. The terrain continues the central North Shore pattern of ravines draining east toward the lake, clay-loam soils, and mature oak and elm canopies. Spring frost typically lingers later than in inland Cook County because of the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on overnight low temperatures, which influences when irrigation systems can be restarted in spring.

The Hubbard Woods area, the village core around Elm Street, and the bluff-edge properties along Sheridan Road feature a mix of Tudor, Georgian, and shingle-style architecture set on large lots with long-established landscaping. Estate-scale outdoor lighting — uplit specimen trees, gated entry illumination, and downlit terraces — is part of the local outdoor design vocabulary, and traditional holiday lighting on the older homes throughout central Winnetka is a recognizable seasonal staple. Brick and bluestone paver driveways and walks are common throughout the older sections and reach the polymeric joint re-sanding window after the first decade or so of freeze–thaw exposure.

cities.name: Lake Geneva
cities.intro:
Lake Geneva is a Walworth County, Wisconsin community on the north shore of Geneva Lake — a destination market that has historically functioned as a weekend-home community for residents of Chicago and Milwaukee. Lakeshore properties along the south shore through Williams Bay and Fontana, and the homes set off the Geneva Lake Shore Path, sit on glacial outwash with high water tables close to the lake and granular fast-draining soils. Spring frost typically lingers later in this area than in inland Wisconsin or Northern Illinois because of the moderating effect of Geneva Lake on overnight low temperatures.

Wisconsin requires a state plumbing license for irrigation system installation, and lawn sprinkler systems in the state must include a reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow assembly. Many properties in the Lake Geneva area function as second homes and benefit from systems designed for owner-absent operation: smart controllers, leak-detection flow sensors, and outdoor lighting on dusk-to-dawn timing. Tudor, shingle-style, and historic estate architecture are concentrated through the Geneva National area and along the lakeshore corridor near Linn Pier — settings where traditional holiday lighting and architectural landscape lighting are a long-standing part of the seasonal landscape.

cities.name: Williams Bay
cities.intro:
Williams Bay is a small Walworth County village on the southwest shore of Geneva Lake, west of Lake Geneva and east of Fontana. The village's footprint is small and almost entirely residential, with lakeside estate properties along the south shore mixed with smaller cottages and year-round homes set further back from the water. The geological pattern continues that of the Geneva Lake basin — glacial outwash with high water tables close to the lake, granular fast-draining soils, and spring frost that lingers later than in inland Wisconsin or Northern Illinois because of Geneva Lake's moderating effect on overnight low temperatures.

Williams Bay sits along the Geneva Lake Shore Path — the 21-mile public footpath circling the lake — and many lakefront properties incorporate the path as a defined boundary at the lakeside edge of the lot. The Yerkes Observatory, on the village's northern edge, anchors a notable concentration of early-20th-century architecture in the surrounding neighborhoods. As elsewhere in the Geneva Lake market, Wisconsin requires a state plumbing license for irrigation system installation, and lawn sprinkler systems must include a reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow assembly.

cities.name: Fontana
cities.intro:
Fontana — officially Fontana-on-Geneva-Lake — is a small Walworth County village on the southwest shore of Geneva Lake, west of Williams Bay. The village is one of the more concentrated estate markets on the lake, with historic mansion-scale properties along the south shore and the Lake Geneva Country Club anchoring the western lakefront. The geology mirrors the rest of the Geneva Lake basin — glacial outwash with high water tables near the lake, granular fast-draining soils, and spring frost lingering later than in inland Wisconsin because of the lake's moderating effect on overnight low temperatures.

The estate properties along the south shore feature long lakefront sightlines, mature canopies, and substantial established landscaping that supports estate-scale outdoor lighting work — uplit specimen trees, illuminated paver paths to the lakefront, and architectural lighting on the historic homes. Many properties function as second homes, and irrigation, lighting, and paver work needs to operate well even with the owner absent for stretches — smart controllers, leak-detection flow sensors, and dusk-to-dawn lighting timing are common defaults. As elsewhere in Wisconsin, the state requires a plumbing license for irrigation installation and an RPZ backflow assembly on every system.
